sentences.text: This is in 1940.
I forgot to mention before that in 1938, November the 2nd-- this just occurred to me-- Hitler and the Hungarian government, they became close friends.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Hungarians on their own, they were anti-Semites on their own style, not what they became later.
Anyway, because of this friendship, Hungary was given back a part of Slovakia, and that included my town too.
Just to-- for the record, in 1919, Hungary lost the war, the First World War, and it was chopped up a little bit.
The northern part, the eastern part became to Romania.
The northern part was a part became a new entity, which is called Czechoslovakia.
And the south, Yugoslavia was created.
And on the north in [INAUDIBLE], even Austria was given a piece.

So because of Hitler was the boss, became the boss of Europe, and they had a-- they call it the Viennese Conference, Ciano and-- Daladier, I don't know whether he was there-- but Hitler and Mussolini and his friends chopped up Czechoslovakia.
Slovakia became a-- a puppet government.
But the southern part of Slovakia, which was occupied by many Hungarian people, was given back to Hungary.
Now all of a sudden we became back Hungarians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Hungary was-- as I said before, was already busy with their anti-Jewish laws.
They did it on their own, but they wanted to please Hitler, because Hitler was-- he wanted to 100%.
They were just going step by step.
First it was the first Jewish law.
Sure enough, when I became about 13 years old, all of a sudden the Hungarian government asked every Jewish businessman to send in their licenses.
In other words, they took away their permit of conducting business with all kinds of excuses.
You-- you are not originally Hungarian.
Your parents were coming from Poland.
You had something-- Anyway, 90% of all the Jewish businesses were shut off.
In my father's case, it was something different.
I'll never forget that.
My father had good connections in Budapest, plus we were real-- he showed it-- he proved that-- that our ancestors were born in Hungary for, who knows, 150, 200 years.
And from Budapest came back the order, Mr.
Greenfeld's junkyard should continue.
You have no right to take away his licenses.
And the local guy from the mayor's office, he was furious.
What?
And he was arguing with Budapest, sending back the license back and forth, back and forth.
Sure enough, my father was called down one day and he took the licenses.
You know what, your honor?
Here, you have it.
Keep it.
And he threw it to his-- on his desk.
This young-- he was assistant to the mayor.
He was actually the vice mayor or secondary mayor, Zolos.
He was-- he wanted to take advantage of this.
And he got mad.
He wanted to send us out those days to Kamianets-Podilskyi.
I don't know whether you ever heard of Kamianets-Podilskyi.
Kamianets-Podilskyi is a town someplace in Ukraine, and this is a grave.
This was a place for about 40,000, 50,000 Hungarian Jews who couldn't prove their identity or their ancestors' identity.
They were called that they are from different-- they did not have citizenship.
They were sent away over there.
And they had to dig their own grave and they were slaughtered there in Kamianets-Podilskyi.

And we were supposed to send out to there-- to that place, except at the border, as we got to the border [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Hungarian police officer, high-- high-ranking officer, said, what are you doing here?
You have-- you are actually kosher.
What's wrong with you?
He says, don't ask me.
We don't--
Anyway, he sent us back over there.
This is in 1941.
So we tasted it already, this what it means.
And saying the truth, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many, many Polish Jews were coming to Hungary escaping.
And they told us stories, horrible stories.
And I will not deny the fact that I am ashamed of it today.
We were so naive.
I'm not talking me as a young boy, but in general the Jewish community and the leadership was naive enough [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they didn't take it seriously.
All these warnings, they came down.
They tell us stories, terrible stories, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they didn't want believe it.
If Budapest or the Jewish leadership would believe those things, then we didn't have to wait 'til 1944 when they came and took us away.
This is-- this is how I feel.
And it's a painful topic to talk about it.
But it was the-- it was-- it was very naive.
The people were very naive.
The Jewish people were always optimistic.
But in this case I think it was more than optimism.
The Hungarian Jews, they thought of themselves that they are-- after all, we are great patriots.
We are important for the government.
They're not going to do to us what they did to the Polish Jews or to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In other words, they were cocksure of themselves that it couldn't happen here.
And unfortunately, when they realized it, it was too late.
And those days, there was plenty, plenty time yet to do something about it.
That was-- that was number one.
They took away the business license.
Jewish doctors, I think-- Jewish boys couldn't attend any more universities.
And the source of parnassah or livelihood shrunk.
So many, many people were supported by the local-- by the local synagogue.
Larger families were-- Jewish people who used to go to their own businesses, all of a sudden they became-- they went out to the wood, to the forest, they're chopping wood.
They did work which was not their profession, just to survive.
I'll never forget that we did something also, a beautiful thing which was-- the credit belongs to my father.
We-- he created a-- a source of parnassah not for us only, a livelihood for probably about four, five families.
He found that-- he had connections with a big factory, and he brought home big, big pieces of carpets.
And from the carpets, we took apart the carpets.
And from those strings in the carpet, each of the carpet, the string actually was twisted from five thin pieces of thread, just like you have a [INAUDIBLE], if I may compare it to it.
And we took that apart, home, in the house.
Boys and girls were doing it.
We took this apart and made-- it was wool.
And we made from these [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wool for knitting, yarn.
From carpets, you took apart the carpets and took it to small pieces and thin pieces.
And we tied it together and it was a beautiful cones of yarns.
And the Jewish mothers were using it for knitting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] caps and scarves for those poor boys who were called in to be sent out to Ukraine.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ukraine was a horrible word to mention, because about 65,000 of the nicest Jewish boys from Hungary, from age 22 to 35 and 40, were called up and sent away over there to Ukraine.
They got this invitation, it was-- SOS it was called, like it was an important call.
And they were not on army duty, but they were just forced labor, helping [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the Hungarian and the German armies.
That was the official name.

Unfortunately, the truth is that the Hungarian Nazi government, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whether they did it on their own or not-- I know after the war I read about-- a book about-- from one of the defense ministers of Hungary, this is most criminal thing, because most of people, most of soldier-- any soldier who is out on the-- in the fire line more than a few months, he is sent home and exchanged.
These boys were sent there not to come back.
That was the purpose.
And about 50,000 or 60,000 were perished over there in the horrible cold.
They were not dressed for it.
They did not have food.
And they were surrounded with Ukrainian, Hungarian butchers, who were looking for every occasion to-- I had two uncles who were-- somebody was telling me, he says, they just froze.
They had hospitals.
And they were-- and there was over there a-- they call it mine.
They were supposed to pick mines.
These mines were connected to each other every few-- maybe 100 meter, 100 yard away, they were connected with-- with wires.
Somebody made a mistake and they all blew up.
Hundreds and hundreds of Jewish boys were killed in this way.
Today it's a well-known fact.
I'm telling you that there are books about this.
This was one of the most horrible page of the Hungarian anti-Semitism was-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH], or forced labor camps.
The ghetto was actually established, as I told you, only the beginning of 1944, when Germans occupied Hungary.
The Hungarians were asked by Hitler to go and-- they were pressuring them to squeeze the Jewish issue.
And the Hungarians were cooperating.
They tried for certain times-- they had some prime ministers who were trying to-- to fool a little bit Hitler.
They were not as fast with these brutalities as the Germans demanded it.
But it was tough.
Sure enough, the Germans were not satisfied what the Hungarians did.
It was not-- they wanted to-- the judenfragen, or the Jewish problem, was not solved yet according to what they would like to have.
Actually, the Germans wanted a total, total destruction or wiping out the Jewish population.
They did this even in 1945, in April, a few weeks before the liberation that they knew that they are losing, they were still looking for to get rid of the remaining Jews.
So this was not-- had-- had nothing to do with the war.
This was a war against the Jews.
And so the ghetto did not start-- coming back to your question, because you asked me about the ghetto.
The ghetto period did not start 'til 1944, early part, March.
I think they came in March 19th.
That was the time when the Germans occupied Budapest.
And they ride away like fire.
They were all over.
They were all over, in every little town.
They-- and then it was going like this.
And unfortunately, Hungarian population was ready for it.
They were looking for the Jewish values, jewelry.
Well, this was-- this was done before.
You were asking me what kind of restrictions.
A Jew was not supposed to have radio, no car, no bicycle, no telephone.
Everything was cut off from him.
It was just like-- how shall I say it?
To be kept totally, totally cut away from everything which we call normal human life.
No schooling.
Then came the ghetto time.
It was already-- most of the husbands, most of the fathers of normal, healthy age, up to about-- up to about 45 or 50, even 55, yes.
Yes.
They took in already people who were grandfathers for forced labor.
All of a sudden, even I remember those days where pious Jews had to go to the train on the Sabbath or on holiday.
I had an uncle in Slovakia who was taken out like this.
And just-- he was so over-- he was so shocked from the fact that he has to travel that he fainted just before the train came in.
And he was chopped up to pieces, one of the biggest scholars in the whole-- in the whole country.
It was my uncle's brother.
This happened from the ghetto.
So every healthy middle-aged man, younger middle-aged male, was already not home.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Now-- now we are up to the point where the ghetto came and we were taken away to a forced labor camp.
You're right.
From the ghetto, we were taken in by a-- by a Hungarian officer.
His name was Horvat.
He came into the ghetto.
And I-- I will have to admit that this man was doing something, a great thing.

Instead of sending us-- he knew that everybody from the ghetto will be sent to Auschwitz, so he was-- he was smart enough or good enough to take out all the manpower was from 14 to 60, young boys from 14 up to 60.
That means most of them were 14 to 19 and 55 to 60, something like that, both extreme groups.
We were called the ghetto battalions.
We were taken in by-- we had to walk in from our town.
That was the last time I saw my-- the rest of my family, my mother and my younger brother, who was a little bit shorter, because this-- this army officer, he just took and-- come on.
You go this way.
You go this way.
Not like Mengele, but just judged it.
My brother was a little bit shorter than I am, about a year younger than me.
I was 16 and 1/2.
He was 15 and 1/2.
And that was the last time I saw them.
We were marching in that same day.
It was late afternoon.
We were-- started to march to a town, which is called Jelsava.
It's in Slovaki, in Slovenska today.
It's spelled Y-O-L-S-V-A.
This was like many, many-- from many, many ghettos, the-- these forced laborers were collected over there.
This is like a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] point where many, many hundreds of people got together.
They had over there a large barracks, army barracks, and it was full of these forced laborers.
And from there, they organized 300, 300, 300, which was called a battalion.
And we were sent out right away, even though we were not what you call the normal age group for the labor going out to the-- to the battlefield.
Somebody made-- twisted it around, because we were-- this was supposed to be-- originally supposed to be they are going for farm work in the country.
But somebody was mixing up these things and the big shots got those positions.
And we were-- we were sent out-- outside of the country to the Russian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fire line, which is called a front, to the front.
That's how we called it, the area which-- where the-- where the battles were, where the battles were fought, that was called the front.
Jelsava we stayed not more than about-- we were doing some local work.
Every day we went out to the forest.
I still met over there some boys, Gentile boys, with whom I went to high school.
And if I would have enough brain or guts, I would tell them, John, give me a place to hide.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But somehow we did not-- we didn't think of it that they are going to take my beautiful mother, who was 44, and my sister, one of them was 18, 19, 21, and 23, and my-- my brother, that they're going to take him and take him away to a slaughter house.
And it somehow didn't penetrate.
It couldn't-- we couldn't think of that.
So-- and I saw everybody was staying, so I stayed.
But looking retroactively, I say to myself, I was a young kid, why shouldn't I-- why didn't I have enough brain or guts to hide someplace?
Some people did.
It wasn't-- and it was sometimes very dangerous.
There were many, many of them were caught and lost their life.
What is the next question, please?
I was asking you what type of work did you do at that forced labor point?
We were taken out to the Russian front, climbing huge mountains.
Huge mountains means we had to pick up ammunitions, 40, 50 kilo-- not pound, kilo-- which equals to about 90 pounds, I would say.
Sometimes two boys with one huge case of ammunition.
And we had to go up not on a straight level, but mountains, between trees.
And it was pulling down and it was somebody fall down.
And-- and actually, we had guides, guards who were pretty brutal.
And we had to schlep this out or carry it out between the two armies, in other words, into the most dangerous spots.
And we were supposed to deliver these.
And you also had to dig ditches for the Hungarian army.
And many, many times we were in very dangerous positions, where the Russians saw us and they-- they were just throwing in mines.
I remember one Saturday afternoon a mine just exploded not far from me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was in-- and we were up on those mountains in Poland, actually it's the other part of the-- other side of the Hungarian border.
The name of this town was Vorokhta and Delyatyn and Mykulychyn.
These were towns which I remember.
And huge mountains, beautiful, high mountains.
But it was such a job to walk up those mountains, I said to myself, never do I-- they are beautiful mountains if you go there now for a-- for a vacation place.
But we were-- we were terribly tired from those-- climbing up those mountains and schlepping.
We also had to take up over there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nailed wires.
I recall this.
Barbed-- barbed wires.
The Hungarian army wanted to keep the Russian tanks-- these are supposed to hold them back.
Naturally, they probably had a good laugh when they saw those barbed wires.
But we had to schlep them and we had to schlep also tress and big, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] heavy loads.
And many, many times it happened that one-- one boy fall down, so the whole load remain on the other one.
This was going on for a few months.
And naturally, the Russians were getting stronger and stronger and we had to step back.
Once it happened that I was doing it for seven days, this schlepping day and night, day and night, no rest, no water.
I remember that I was drinking water from a-- you know, how the horse shoe when they step, they leave a little ditch.
And then the rain falls so the water remains in that little ditch.
And I was drinking that water, because I was totally dried out.
And I said to myself, that's it.
I-- I just don't-- I don't-- I was so exhausted that I said to myself, I don't care.
And I left everything, threw away my-- my tools.
And I just came down from the big mountain into a valley.
It was Friday night.
I took some branches from pine trees, put it under me and put another piece, put it on top of me.
And I said, I must sleep.
I must-- I was so exhausted.
I must have a rest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the morning I woke up.
I was even saying some prayers.
It was Friday night.
I was singing my [HEBREW] a little bit, but I fall asleep like a-- like a-- like a dead man.
In the morning, I woke up and I was surrounded by dead Russian soldiers.
In other words, I went into a territory which I was not supposed to be.
I climbed back.
And everybody says, where are you, little Greenfeld?
They are looking for you.
You know, you will be court-marshaled.
You're going-- they're going to take you to the-- to the-- to the military court.
You were leaving your place of work without permission and you'll be ready to be shot.
This is what the Jewish army commander told me.
And people came to say goodbye to me.
They are going to take you out there and boom.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how I felt.
I cannot even describe it.
But sure enough, the Russians gave us a big push and everybody was running like-- like crazy.

We had to-- we had to-- the Russians came with big strengthened, and the whole-- the whole group, we had to move back toward-- toward the west, coming back to the-- back to the Hungarian-- over the Hungarian border.
This was about, I would say, about not more than about 100 to 150 kilometers on the outside of the Hungarian border.
And now we were pushed back and came back to the mainland or to Hungary.
OK.
